item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Excellent entry overall, but an you explain your reasoning behind classifying it as "Safe" instead of Euclid?"

At this point, it has been established that a spirit (or spirits) has been bound to the aqueduct, and is actively causing mischief. Its effects are being managed, but the cost of simply diverting a river would be extraordinary, not to mention the cost associated with filtering and diluting a minimum of 108 million liters of water every single month. According to canon, Euclid-class anomalies get far more funding than Safe-class ones, so arguably cost alone should drive the classification.

There are, however, three more reasons I believe it should be Euclid. To avoid this being a wall of text, I will be brief. The first is that the spirit is at the very least sentient; the second is that the scale of containment and the anomaly's effects create a great risk for a breach of secrecy; and the third is that an earthquake caused the current effects and it is quite possible that another earthquake could exacerbate the anomaly.

item.title: Re: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded: Not a fan of this specific edit—while they are both examples of anomalous Roman architecture, they're separated by 300 years, and 2513 is made of stone masonry while 2766 is made of concrete, so there's no reason they would be made of the same material.

item.title: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Edit: vetoed by author

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ show block
– hide block

This was, apparently, constructed by the Roman Republic. So was SCP-2513. I propose linking these two SCPs together by adding another sentence to the end of the Description.

Editing this:

The section of the Aqua Claudia which includes SCP-2766 was built in 38 CE under the emperor Caligula, and the remainder of the aqueduct was completed in 52 CE by his successor Claudius; 10 years after its completion, due to SCP-2766's effects, the Aqua Claudia was abandoned. In 70 CE, under the emperor Vespasian, containment of SCP-2766 was established by the Praetorian Office of Secret Wisdom4 and a secondary channel of the Aqua Claudia bypassing SCP-2766 was created; after the fall of the Western Roman Empire, SCP-2766 was uncontained until the Vatican Holy Office of Secrets and Prophecy reestablished containment in 786 CE.

To this:

The section of the Aqua Claudia which includes SCP-2766 was built in 38 CE under the emperor Caligula, and the remainder of the aqueduct was completed in 52 CE by his successor Claudius; 10 years after its completion, due to SCP-2766's effects, the Aqua Claudia was abandoned. In 70 CE, under the emperor Vespasian, containment of SCP-2766 was established by the Praetorian Office of Secret Wisdom4 and a secondary channel of the Aqua Claudia bypassing SCP-2766 was created; after the fall of the Western Roman Empire, SCP-2766 was uncontained until the Vatican Holy Office of Secrets and Prophecy reestablished containment in 786 CE.

SCP-2766 is made up of the exact same material as SCP-2513. The remainder of Aqua Claudia is not made up of this material. The reason for this is unknown.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well researched, well conceived, and well written. An unhesitant +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The effect itself is all right, mostly enjoyed it for all the history, but I am so down for ancient Roman Foundation analogues. :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Like I said when this was but a wee draft in the forums, I really dig it.

The idea of spirits getting pissed off that they are underemployed and pulling bloodwine shenanigans is hilarious and weird, and the SCPF equivalents from ancient Rome and the Vatican add a degree of depth to this article that is enjoyable.

+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I always love this sort of "Engineer's nightmare" skip.

I also love that connection to Caligula. I can just picture the conversation…

Manius: Your worship, after much research I have entreated the spirits of the underworld to alter realty that your fountains might produce any liquid you desire!
Caligula: Ya cool, let everybody know me, Lepilla, Cervina, and some of the guys are gonna go cruise the lake for a few days. Send a couple carts of wine and pork rinds if we're gone more than a week. And oh yeah, some of the green stuff cause Cervina's a vegan.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm giving you a +1 because this is utterly bizarre and yet I can absolutely see Caligula doing this.

That being said, you made me imagine wine mixed with period blood, and I hate1 you for that.

Footnotes
1. I don't actually hate you

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love the idea of ancient Foundation-like groups existing, that combined with how strange this is merits a +1. Good work!

item.title: (no title)
